_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 10-6 Mass Casualty Incidents (MDHHS Reviewed 2018-10-26). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Special Operations Protocol Section 10-6: Mass Casualty Incidents — MDHHS Reviewed 2018
purpose: Establish EMS response framework for Mass Casualty Incidents (MCI) including SALT triage, ICS integration, treatment sector operations, and patient transport coordination in Michigan.

sections.title: MCI Definition and Activation
content: Mass Casualty Incident (MCI): any incident which, because of physical size, number and criticality of victims, or complexity, is likely to overwhelm the local resources typically available
MCI is a resource and complexity problem — not a fixed casualty count
If MCI is detected, immediately declare to dispatch and assure hospitals and other stakeholders are made aware
For major incidents, including incidents involving multiple counties/MCA resources, RMCC should be alerted

sections.title: DISASTER Paradigm
content: D — Detection: recognize that an incident has occurred; identify scope and hazard type
I — Incident Command: establish ICS; designate Incident Commander; integrate EMS Branch
S — Safety/Security: establish scene safety perimeter; identify hazards; ensure responder safety
A — Assess Hazards: identify threats to responders and patients (CBRNE, structural, secondary device)
S — Support: identify needed resources; activate mutual aid; request additional assets
T — Triage/Treatment: initiate SALT triage; establish treatment sectors; begin life-saving interventions
E — Evacuation: coordinate patient transport to appropriate hospitals via Transport Unit Leader
R — Recovery: demobilization; documentation; resource restocking; critical incident debriefing

sections.title: ICS — EMS Branch Structure
content: INCIDENT COMMANDER (IC): overall scene command; may be fire, law enforcement, or EMS
EMS BRANCH DIRECTOR: commands all EMS operations at incident
TRIAGE UNIT LEADER: oversees triage sector; assigns triage personnel; tracks triage counts
TREATMENT UNIT LEADER: oversees Red/Yellow/Green treatment sectors; coordinates medical care
— Red Treatment Sector (Immediate): immediate life-threatening injuries requiring immediate intervention
— Yellow Treatment Sector (Delayed): serious but stable injuries; can tolerate brief delay
— Green Treatment Sector (Minor): minor injuries; ambulatory patients ('walking wounded')
TRANSPORT UNIT LEADER: coordinates hospital destinations; tracks patients transported; interfaces with RMCC/CHECC
STAGING OFFICER: manages ambulance and personnel staging
REHABILITATION OFFICER: manages responder rehab and rest area

sections.title: SALT Triage Algorithm
content: SALT: Sort — Assess — Life Saving Interventions — Treatment/Transport
STEP 1 — SORT (Global Sorting):
— Command: 'If you can walk, move to [Green area]' — ambulatory patients → GREEN
— Remaining: wave hand or follow commands → YELLOW (delayed)
— Still/not responding → assess individually
STEP 2 — ASSESS (Individual Assessment of non-ambulatory patients):
— Assess in order: Still patients first (highest priority), then non-ambulatory but responsive
STEP 3 — LIFE SAVING INTERVENTIONS (LSI) — perform ONLY these before assigning category:
— Control major hemorrhage (tourniquet, wound packing)
— Open airway; if child, provide 2 rescue breaths
— 2 rescue breaths for children only (not adults in SALT)
— Chest decompression as needed (Paramedic only)
— Auto-injectors for nerve agent (CHEMPACK — Protocol 10-4)
STEP 4 — ASSIGN TRIAGE CATEGORY:
— RED (Immediate): life-threatening injury with high likelihood of survival with immediate intervention; breathing present after LSI
— YELLOW (Delayed): serious injury but stable; breathing present; no immediate life threat
— GREEN (Minor): minor injuries; ambulatory; 'walking wounded'

— GRAY (Expectant): injuries incompatible with survival even with maximal intervention; or requires resources that would deprive salvageable patients; breathing NOT present after LSI in adult; still receive comfort care
— BLACK (Dead/Deceased): no respirations after airway repositioning (and 2 breaths in children); pulseless; obvious death

sections.title: Triage Category Definitions
content: RED — Immediate: life-threatening conditions with likely survivability; examples: tension pneumothorax, severe hemorrhage, airway compromise — responds to LSI
YELLOW — Delayed: serious injuries but can tolerate brief delay; examples: long bone fractures, burns <30% BSA, moderate hemorrhage controlled
GREEN — Minor: minor injuries; ambulatory; minimal care required; examples: lacerations, contusions, mild burns

GRAY — Expectant: expected to die regardless of intervention OR injuries require resources disproportionate to likely outcome; may receive comfort care; examples: massive burns (>60%), multi-system trauma without vitals, penetrating brain injury
BLACK — Dead/Deceased: confirmed dead; no breathing after LSI; obvious fatal injuries
NOTE: GRAY category is ethically and legally authorized during declared MCI — resources must benefit the greatest number of salvageable patients

sections.title: Non-Ambulance Transport — MCL 333.20939
content: Non-licensed vehicles may be used for emergency transport when licensed ambulances are not readily available
Non-licensed vehicles include, but are not limited to, wheelchair vans, buses, and other public safety vehicles
If an ambulance operation cannot respond within a reasonable time, exceptional circumstances may allow spontaneous use of another vehicle without charge or fee as a humane service under MCL 333.20939
EMS personnel who transport or decide to transport by non-licensed vehicle must file a written report describing the incident with the Medical Control Authority
Licensed EMS personnel should accompany injured patients in non-licensed vehicles whenever possible

sections.title: RMCC and CHECC — Regional and State Coordination
content: RMCC: Regional Medical Coordination Center — regional multi-agency coordination center and single regional point of contact for healthcare resource coordination
CHECC: Community Health Emergency Coordination Center — statewide multi-agency coordination entity for state-level healthcare and public health resources
MCL 333.20965: provides liability immunity for RMCC and CHECC coordination decisions made in good faith during declared emergencies
Transport Unit Leader interfaces with RMCC for hospital destination decisions — do NOT self-select hospitals without RMCC coordination in MCI
RMCC and CHECC have authority to divert patients from closest facility to prevent overwhelming single hospital

sections.title: Treatment Sector Operations
content: RED TREATMENT SECTOR: ALS providers; most critical patients; prioritize: airway, hemorrhage, decompression
YELLOW TREATMENT SECTOR: ALS or BLS; reassess RED patients who improve or worsen; prepare for transport
GREEN TREATMENT SECTOR: BLS or first aid providers; ambulatory patients; triage for treatment
GRAY SECTOR: separate from treatment areas; comfort measures only; do NOT consume scarce resources
Reassess categories continuously — patients may upgrade or downgrade
Track all patients with triage tags or written log — patient tracking is critical for accountability

sections.title: Documentation and Patient Tracking
content: Triage tags: apply to all patients; record category, interventions, patient identifier, and destination
Transport Unit Leader: maintain running log of patients transported, receiving hospital, and EMS unit
EMS agencies must submit MCI documentation to MCA within 24 hours
NIMS/ICS compliance required — use standard ICS forms (ICS 214, ICS 205A) as available

key_drugs: Tourniquets — first-line hemorrhage control in SALT triage LSI phase
Nerve agent antidote auto-injectors — SALT LSI for mass nerve agent exposure (Protocol 10-3/10-4)
deterioration_flags: SALT triage reveals greater than expected RED/GRAY patients — activate additional resources immediately; notify RMCC
Hospital capacity exceeded — contact RMCC for distribution guidance; activate CHECC if regional capacity threatened
CBRNE agent identified at MCI scene — halt triage; protect responders; activate CBRNE protocols 10-1 through 10-5 as appropriate
scope_notes: SALT triage is the preferred Michigan MCI triage method; other triage systems must comply with MUCC. The GRAY (Expectant) category is used when patients are unlikely to survive given available resources. Non-licensed vehicle transport may be used when licensed ambulances are not readily available and requires written reporting to the MCA under MCL 333.20939. RMCC is the regional coordination entity; CHECC is the Community Health Emergency Coordination Center.
